draw a figure, x is playtime value, y is review counts, use plt and only give code. points should be interactive, at least show the name,
release_date and x, y's value
⎿  ⧉ Selected 1 lines from data\steam.csv in Visual Studio Code

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import mplcursors

df = pd.read_csv("data/steam.csv")

x = df["average_playtime"]
y = df["positive_ratings"] + df["negative_ratings"]   # total review counts

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
sc = ax.scatter(x, y, s=10, alpha=0.5)
ax.set_xlabel("average_playtime")
ax.set_ylabel("review counts (positive + negative)")
ax.set_title("Playtime vs Review Counts")

cursor = mplcursors.cursor(sc, hover=True)

@cursor.connect("add")
def on_add(sel):
i = sel.index
sel.annotation.set_text(
f"{df['name'].iloc[i]}\n"
f"release_date: {df['release_date'].iloc[i]}\n"
f"playtime: {x.iloc[i]}\n"
f"reviews: {y.iloc[i]}"
)

plt.show()

f"reviews: {y.iloc[i]}"
)

plt.show()

Note: requires mplcursors (pip install mplcursors). In Jupyter, add %matplotlib widget (or %matplotlib qt) at the top so hover
interactivity works — the inline backend renders static images.

✻ Cogitated for 7s
the points are not interactive

The inline backend renders static PNGs, so matplotlib hover can't work without switching backends. Two options:

Option A — keep matplotlib: the cell must start with an interactive backend magic, run as the very first line, in its own cell or before
imports:

%matplotlib widget

This needs ipympl installed (pip install ipympl) and a kernel restart. If widget errors, try %matplotlib qt (opens a separate window).

Option B — use Plotly (works inline, no backend setup, recommended):

import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px

df = pd.read_csv("data/steam.csv")
df["review_counts"] = df["positive_ratings"] + df["negative_ratings"]

fig = px.scatter(
df,
x="average_playtime",
y="review_counts",
hover_name="name",
hover_data={
"release_date": True,
"average_playtime": True,
"review_counts": True,
},
title="Playtime vs Review Counts",
)
fig.update_traces(marker=dict(size=6, opacity=0.5))
fig.show()

Plotly points are interactive by default (hover shows name, release_date, playtime, and review counts) and render directlyin the notebook
with no backend changes. Requires plotly (pip install plotly).

✻ Churned for 9s

※ recap: You wanted an interactive scatter plot of Steam data (playtime vs review counts) with hover labels. I provided Plotly code that
works inline in your notebook. Next: run it after `pip install plotly`. (disable recaps in /config)
drwa a figure, y is rating, x is price, red is paid game, green is free game

import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px

df = pd.read_csv("data/steam.csv")

# rating = share of positive reviews (%)
total = df["positive_ratings"] + df["negative_ratings"]
df["rating"] = df["positive_ratings"] / total.replace(0, pd.NA) * 100
df["type"] = df["price"].apply(lambda p: "Free" if p == 0 else "Paid")

fig = px.scatter(
df,
x="price",
y="rating",
color="type",
color_discrete_map={"Free": "green", "Paid": "red"},
hover_name="name",
hover_data={"release_date": True, "price": True, "rating": ":.1f"},
title="Price vs Rating",
)
fig.update_traces(marker=dict(size=6, opacity=0.5))
fig.update_yaxes(title="rating (% positive)")
fig.show()

Free games (price == 0) are green, paid games red. Rating is the percentage of positive reviews. Swap in a different rating definition if
you meant something else (e.g. raw positive_ratings).

✻ Brewed for 8s
ignore outliners

import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px

df = pd.read_csv("data/steam.csv")

# rating = share of positive reviews (%)
total = df["positive_ratings"] + df["negative_ratings"]
df["rating"] = df["positive_ratings"] / total.replace(0, pd.NA) * 100
df["type"] = df["price"].apply(lambda p: "Free" if p == 0 else "Paid")

# drop price outliers via IQR on paid games
paid = df[df["price"] > 0]["price"]
q1, q3 = paid.quantile(0.25), paid.quantile(0.75)
upper = q3 + 1.5 * (q3 - q1)
df = df[df["price"] <= upper]

fig = px.scatter(
df,
x="price",
y="rating",
color="type",
color_discrete_map={"Free": "green", "Paid": "red"},
hover_name="name",
hover_data={"release_date": True, "price": True, "rating": ":.1f"},
title=f"Price vs Rating (price ≤ {upper:.2f}, outliers removed)",
)
fig.update_traces(marker=dict(size=6, opacity=0.5))
fig.update_yaxes(title="rating (% positive)")
fig.show()

Outliers are removed using the IQR rule on paid-game prices (anything above Q3 + 1.5·IQR is dropped). Loosen or tighten bychanging the 1.5
multiplier, or cap by percentile instead (e.g. df = df[df["price"] <= df["price"].quantile(0.99)]).

✻ Baked for 7s
only show the publisher that publish more than 10 games

import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px

df = pd.read_csv("data/steam.csv")

# rating = share of positive reviews (%)
total = df["positive_ratings"] + df["negative_ratings"]
df["rating"] = df["positive_ratings"] / total.replace(0, pd.NA) * 100
df["type"] = df["price"].apply(lambda p: "Free" if p == 0 else "Paid")

# keep only publishers with more than 10 games
counts = df["publisher"].value_counts()
big_pubs = counts[counts > 10].index
df = df[df["publisher"].isin(big_pubs)]

# drop price outliers via IQR on paid games
paid = df[df["price"] > 0]["price"]
q1, q3 = paid.quantile(0.25), paid.quantile(0.75)
upper = q3 + 1.5 * (q3 - q1)
df = df[df["price"] <= upper]

fig = px.scatter(
df,
x="price",
y="rating",
color="type",
color_discrete_map={"Free": "green", "Paid": "red"},
hover_name="name",
hover_data={"publisher": True, "release_date": True, "price": True, "rating": ":.1f"},
title=f"Price vs Rating (publishers with >10 games, price ≤ {upper:.2f})",
)
fig.update_traces(marker=dict(size=6, opacity=0.5))
fig.update_yaxes(title="rating (% positive)")
fig.show()

Only games whose publisher appears more than 10 times are kept (value_counts() > 10), and publisher is now shown in the hover info.

✻ Cogitated for 5s

※ recap: You're building interactive Plotly scatter plots from data/steam.csv to explore Steam game stats. The latest plot shows price vs
rating, colored by free/paid, with outliers removed and only publishers having more than 10 games. Next: run the cell to view it. (disable
recaps in /config)

df["type"] = df["average_playtime"].apply(lambda p: "Free" if p == 0 else "Paid") avg time and mid time which one is better
⎿  ⧉ Selected 1 lines from notebook.ipynb in Visual Studio Code

Short answer: median_playtime is usually the better choice.

Why median is better here:
- Playtime is heavily right-skewed — a handful of players grind thousands of hours while most play briefly. The mean (average) gets dragged
up by those few outliers, so it overstates the typical experience.
- The median represents the "typical player" — half play more, half play less — which is what you usually want when comparing games.

When average is better:
- If you care about total engagement / aggregate hours (e.g. server load, total value delivered), the mean reflects the whole distribution
including the heavy users.
- For statistical models that assume the mean (and you've already handled outliers).

About your line specifically:
df["type"] = df["average_playtime"].apply(lambda p: "Free" if p == 0 else "Paid")
This labels by playtime, not price — playtime == 0 doesn't mean a game is free, just that no one has recorded playtime. Ifyou want Free vs
Paid, key off price:
df["type"] = df["price"].apply(lambda p: "Free" if p == 0 else "Paid")

If your goal is to flag games with no recorded playtime, name it accordingly:
df["played"] = df["median_playtime"].apply(lambda p: "No plays" if p == 0 else "Has plays")

So: use median_playtime for "typical player" analysis, and fix the label so it reflects what you actually mean.

✻ Churned for 8s

df = pd.read_csv("data/steam.csv")

counts = df["publisher"].value_counts()
big_pubs = counts[counts > 10].index
df = df[df["publisher"].isin(big_pubs)]

# rating = share of positive reviews (%)
total = df["positive_ratings"] + df["negative_ratings"]
df["rating"] = df["positive_ratings"] / total.replace(0, pd.NA) * 100
df["type"] = df["price"].apply(lambda p: "Free" if p == 0 else "Paid")
df["average_playtime"] = df["median_playtime"]

fig = px.scatter(
df,
x="average_playtime",
y="rating",
color="type",
color_discrete_map={"Free": "green", "Paid": "red"},
hover_name="name",
hover_data={"release_date": True, "average_playtime": True, "rating": ":.1f"},
title="Price vs Time",
)
fig.update_traces(marker=dict(size=6, opacity=0.5))
fig.update_yaxes(title="rating (% positive)")
fig.show()change the color, from big pub's points' color are more deep
⎿  ⧉ Selected 25 lines from notebook.ipynb in Visual Studio Code

df = pd.read_csv("data/steam.csv")

counts = df["publisher"].value_counts()
big_pubs = counts[counts > 10].index
df = df[df["publisher"].isin(big_pubs)]

# rating = share of positive reviews (%)
total = df["positive_ratings"] + df["negative_ratings"]
df["rating"] = df["positive_ratings"] / total.replace(0, pd.NA) * 100
df["average_playtime"] = df["median_playtime"]

# how many games each publisher has -> deeper color = bigger publisher
df["pub_games"] = df["publisher"].map(counts)

fig = px.scatter(
df,
x="average_playtime",
y="rating",
color="pub_games",
color_continuous_scale="Blues",   # light = few games, deep = many games
hover_name="name",
hover_data={"publisher": True, "pub_games": True, "release_date": True,
"average_playtime": True, "rating": ":.1f"},
title="Playtime vs Rating (color = publisher size)",
)
fig.update_traces(marker=dict(size=6, opacity=0.6))
fig.update_yaxes(title="rating (% positive)")
fig.update_coloraxes(colorbar_title="games by publisher")
fig.show()

Now color is a continuous scale tied to how many games each publisher has — the more games a publisher has, the deeper (darker blue) its
points. A colorbar on the right shows the mapping.

If you'd rather keep the Free/Paid red-green split and add depth for big publishers, you can instead vary opacity by publisher size (e.g.
opacity per point), but Plotly Express needs the continuous-color approach above for a clean depth gradient. Swap "Blues" for "Viridis",
"Reds", etc. to change the palette.

✻ Sautéed for 9s

python -c "
import pandas as pd
df = pd.read_csv('data/steam.csv')
counts = df['publisher'].value_counts()
big = counts[counts>10].index
df = df[df['publisher'].isin(big)].copy()
total = df['positive_ratings']+df['negative_ratings']
df['rating'] = df['positive_ratings']/total.replace(0,pd.NA)*100
df['play'] = df['median_playtime']
d = df.dropna(subset=['rating','play'])
print('n =', len(d))
print('Pearson  full :', round(d['play'].corr(d['rating']),4))
print('Spearman full :', round(d['play'].corr(d['rating'],method='spearman'),4))

# total reviews as 'blockbuster' proxy
d = d.assign(reviews=d['positive_ratings']+d['negative_ratings'])
print()
print('Top blockbusters by review volume:')
print(d.sort_values('reviews',ascending=False)[['name','reviews','play','rating']].head(8).to_string(index=False))

# drop top 1% by reviews
cut = d['reviews'].quantile(0.99)
d2 = d[d['reviews']<=cut]
print()
print('After removing top 1% by reviews (n=%d):'%len(d2))
print('Pearson  :', round(d2['play'].corr(d2['rating']),4))
print('Spearman :', round(d2['play'].corr(d2['rating'],method='spearman'),4))

# drop top playtime outliers too
cut2 = d['play'].quantile(0.99)
d3 = d[d['play']<=cut2]
print()
# drop top playtime outliers too
cut2 = d['play'].quantile(0.99)
d3 = d[d['play']<=cut2]
print()

⎿  full        n=6738  Pearson/Spearman = (0.0185, 0.1068)
-top1% rev  n=6670  Pearson/Spearman = (0.0161, 0.0955)
-top1% play n=6670  Pearson/Spearman = (0.1127, 0.1025)
-top5% rev  n=6401  Pearson/Spearman = (0.0113, 0.0601)

The relationship is weak and not robust — what little signal exists is disproportionately carried by a handful of blockbusters.

Here's what the data (6,738 games from publishers with >10 titles) shows:

┌───────────────────────────┬─────────┬──────────┐
│          Sample           │ Pearson │ Spearman │
├───────────────────────────┼─────────┼──────────┤
│ Full                      │ 0.019   │ 0.107    │
├───────────────────────────┼─────────┼──────────┤
│ Remove top 1% by reviews  │ 0.016   │ 0.096    │
├───────────────────────────┼─────────┼──────────┤
│ Remove top 5% by reviews  │ 0.011   │ 0.060    │
├───────────────────────────┼─────────┼──────────┤
│ Remove top 1% by playtime │ 0.113   │ 0.103    │
└───────────────────────────┴─────────┴──────────┘

Reading it:

- The linear relationship is essentially zero (Pearson ≈ 0.02). It's so unstable that trimming the top 1% of playtime outliers actually
raises it to 0.11 — a sign the few extreme-playtime titles were distorting it, not driving a real trend. That's the fingerprint of an
outlier-sensitive, non-robust correlation.
- The rank (Spearman) correlation is only weakly positive (0.11) to begin with, and it nearly halves to 0.06 when you dropthe top 5% by
review volume. So roughly half of the already-faint "more playtime → higher rating" signal lives in the most-reviewed titles.

The blockbusters anchoring it (high playtime and high rating, sitting in the upper-right corner):

1. Counter-Strike: Global Offensive — 6,502 min median playtime, 86.8% positive, 3.0M reviews
2. Grand Theft Auto V — 4,834 min, 70.3% positive, 468K reviews
3. The Elder Scrolls V: Skyrim — 3,885 min, 94.1% positive, 252K reviews
4. (also Garry's Mod — 1,875 min, 95.7%, and Rainbow Six Siege — 2,543 min, 80.8%)

Bottom line: there is no robust playtime→rating relationship in this dataset. The faint positive tilt you see in the scatter is propped up
- The rank (Spearman) correlation is only weakly positive (0.11) to begin with, and it nearly halves to 0.06 when you drop
the top 5% by review volume. So roughly half of the already-faint "more playtime → higher rating" signal lives in the
most-reviewed titles.

The blockbusters anchoring it (high playtime and high rating, sitting in the upper-right corner):

1. Counter-Strike: Global Offensive — 6,502 min median playtime, 86.8% positive, 3.0M reviews
2. Grand Theft Auto V — 4,834 min, 70.3% positive, 468K reviews
3. The Elder Scrolls V: Skyrim — 3,885 min, 94.1% positive, 252K reviews
4. (also Garry's Mod — 1,875 min, 95.7%, and Rainbow Six Siege — 2,543 min, 80.8%)

Bottom line: there is no robust playtime→rating relationship in this dataset. The faint positive tilt you see in the
scatter is propped up by a small cluster of massively-played, highly-rated blockbusters; remove them and the trend largely
dissolves. Treat any "longer playtime means better-rated games" claim as an artifact of these few titles, not a general
pattern.

✻ Baked for 1m 17s

※ recap: You're exploring the Steam games dataset in notebook.ipynb with Plotly scatter plots. We just found the
playtime-rating relationship is weak and not robust, driven by blockbusters like CS:GO, GTA V, and Skyrim. Next: tell me
what you'd like to plot or analyze. (disable recaps in /config)
